* identify the main chemical elements in the human body;
* describe and draw basic atomic structure;
* identify the differences between atoms, ions, molecules and compounds;
* distinguish the difference between ionic and covalent bonds;
* define a chemical reaction and identify the difference between an endergonic and exergonic reaction;
* define energy and the units of energy;
* describe the role of ATP within the cell;
* describe the common forms of chemical reactions including synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation and oxidation/reduction reactions;
* outline the functions of water and the chemical property underpinning water's ability to act as a solvent;
* define the terms mole, molar and molarity;
* define the terms acid, base and salt;
* draw and define the pH scale and describe what is meant by the terms acidic and alkaline;
* describe basic cell structure with particular emphasis to the plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm and mitochondria.

---

## Definition

A foundational chapter outlining the basic chemistry and cellular structures relevant to sport and exercise biochemistry. It covers the chemical elements of the human body, atomic structure, the distinctions between atoms, ions, molecules and compounds, the nature of chemical bonds and reactions, the energetics underpinning metabolism, the properties of water, acid–base chemistry, and basic cell architecture.

## Core Principles

- The human body is composed of a defined set of main chemical elements, organised into atoms with a basic atomic structure (protons, neutrons, electrons).
- Atoms, ions, molecules and compounds are distinct entities differentiated by charge state and bonding.
- Chemical bonds include ionic bonds (transfer of electrons) and covalent bonds (sharing of electrons).
- A chemical reaction transforms reactants into products; reactions are classified energetically as endergonic (energy-absorbing) or exergonic (energy-releasing).
- Energy is the capacity to do work, measured in defined units (e.g. joules, calories).
- ATP serves as the cell's primary energy currency, coupling exergonic to endergonic processes.
- Common reaction types include synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation, and oxidation/reduction reactions.
- Water's polarity underpins its role as a biological solvent and supports its multiple physiological functions.
- Acids, bases and salts are defined by their behaviour in aqueous solution; the pH scale quantifies acidity and alkalinity.
- Concentration is expressed using the terms mole, molar and molarity.
- Basic cell structure is organised around the plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm and mitochondria.

- Water as a solvent
- Mole, molar, molarity
- Acids, bases, salts
- pH scale; acidic vs. alkaline
- Cell structure: plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria

## Practical Applications

- Understanding atomic and molecular chemistry provides the basis for interpreting metabolic pathways in sport and exercise.
- Knowledge of ATP and exergonic/endergonic coupling underpins analysis of energy systems during exercise.
- Recognising oxidation/reduction and phosphorylation/dephosphorylation reactions supports the study of cellular respiration and substrate metabolism.
- Awareness of water's solvent properties and acid–base balance (pH) informs hydration, electrolyte handling, and buffering during physical activity.
- Familiarity with cell structure (plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria) supports understanding of cellular adaptations to training.
